From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [A.C. Crispin](/w/index.php?title=A.C._Crispin&redirect=no "A.C. Crispin"))

American science fiction writer

| Ann C. Crispin | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | Ann Carol Tickell  (1950-04-05)April 5, 1950  [Stamford, Connecticut](/wiki/Stamford,_Connecticut "Stamford, Connecticut"), U.S. |
| Died | September 6, 2013(2013-09-06) (aged 63)  [Waldorf, Maryland](/wiki/Waldorf,_Maryland "Waldorf, Maryland"), U.S. |
| Pen name | A. C. Crispin |
| Occupation | Writer, Computer Programmer[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1) |
| Alma mater | [University of Maryland](/wiki/University_of_Maryland "University of Maryland")[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1) |
| Period | 1983–2013 |
| Genre | Science fiction, screen [novelization](/wiki/Novelization "Novelization") |
| Notable works | *[Yesterday's Son](/wiki/Yesterday%27s_Son "Yesterday's Son")*, *[Sarek](/wiki/Sarek_(novel) "Sarek (novel)")*, *[The Han Solo Trilogy](/wiki/The_Han_Solo_Trilogy "The Han Solo Trilogy")* |
| Notable awards | Tie-in Writers Grandmaster |
| Spouse | Randy Crispin (divorced)  [Michael Capobianco](/wiki/Michael_Capobianco "Michael Capobianco") (m. 2001) |
| Website | |
| [accrispin.com](http://accrispin.com) | |

**Ann Carol Crispin** (April 5, 1950 – September 6, 2013) was an American science fiction writer and the author of 23 published novels. She wrote several *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")* and *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* novelizations; she also created an original science fiction series called *StarBridge*.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ann_C._Crispin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

Crispin started writing in 1978,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and her career began in 1983. As a writer of "[tie-in](/wiki/Tie-in "Tie-in")" novels she was known for developing the backgrounds and emotional lives of on-screen characters.[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1)

Two of her *Star Trek* novels—*[Yesterday's Son](/wiki/Yesterday%27s_Son "Yesterday's Son")* and *[Time for Yesterday](/wiki/Time_for_Yesterday "Time for Yesterday")*—were direct sequels to the third-season episode "[All Our Yesterdays](/wiki/All_Our_Yesterdays_(Star_Trek:_The_Original_Series) "All Our Yesterdays (Star Trek: The Original Series)")", and detail Spock and Zarabeth's son.[[3]](#cite_note-3) *Yesterday's Son* was the first non-novelization Star Trek novel to appear on the [*New York Times* Best Seller list](/wiki/New_York_Times_Best_Seller_list "New York Times Best Seller list").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Her later *Star Trek* works included the novel *[Sarek](/wiki/Sarek_(novel) "Sarek (novel)")*, which takes place after *[Star Trek VI: The Undiscovered Country](/wiki/Star_Trek_VI:_The_Undiscovered_Country "Star Trek VI: The Undiscovered Country")*. Her best-known *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* work, *[The Han Solo Trilogy](/wiki/The_Han_Solo_Trilogy "The Han Solo Trilogy")*, chronicles the life of [Han Solo](/wiki/Han_Solo "Han Solo") prior to *[Star Wars Episode IV: A New Hope](/wiki/Star_Wars_Episode_IV:_A_New_Hope "Star Wars Episode IV: A New Hope")*.[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1) Crispin also wrote the novelizations for [The *V* miniseries](/wiki/V_(1983_miniseries) "V (1983 miniseries)")[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1) and the film *[Alien Resurrection](/wiki/Alien_Resurrection "Alien Resurrection")*, as well as *[Sylvester](/wiki/Sylvester_(film) "Sylvester (film)")*, a girl and horse film starring Melissa Gilbert.[[5]](#cite_note-APObit-5)

When an editor working for [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") was seeking an author to write a novel dealing with the backstory of [Captain Jack Sparrow](/wiki/Jack_Sparrow "Jack Sparrow"), a major character from the *[Pirates of the Caribbean](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean "Pirates of the Caribbean")* franchise, they contacted Crispin's agent and contracted her to write the book after reading *The Han Solo Trilogy*, which focused on Han Solo's backstory.[[6]](#cite_note-6) According to Crispin herself, it took her three years to write and she did a lot of research on the historical period and the nautical stuff. She was also given the script for *[At World's End](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_At_World%27s_End "Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End")* before the film released, but her book was finished before the script for *[On Stranger Tides](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_On_Stranger_Tides "Pirates of the Caribbean: On Stranger Tides")* was written.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The instructions for A. C. Crispin in writing *[Pirates of the Caribbean: The Price of Freedom](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_The_Price_of_Freedom "Pirates of the Caribbean: The Price of Freedom")* were to "stick to historical fact, unless it conflicts with established Pirates of the Caribbean continuity." Crispin made a faithful effort to do this, having done plenty of research, with *Under the Black Flag* by [David Cordingly](/wiki/David_Cordingly "David Cordingly") being one of the four pirate-related books she found herself using the most consistently.[[9]](#cite_note-9) When releasing the fifth excerpt of her book, Crispin spoke of how she was privileged to write the scene where Han first beheld—and fell for—the *[Millennium Falcon](/wiki/Millennium_Falcon "Millennium Falcon")* in *The Han Solo Trilogy*. She assured fans it was every bit as thrilling to write the scene with Jack Sparrow and the [merchant ship](/wiki/Merchant_ship "Merchant ship") *[Wicked Wench](/wiki/Black_Pearl_(Pirates_of_the_Caribbean) "Black Pearl (Pirates of the Caribbean)")*.[[10]](#cite_note-TPoF_Excerpt_5-10) *The Price of Freedom* was published on May 17, 2011.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

She also created her own StarBridge series of novels, aimed primarily at young readers.[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1)

Crispin was Eastern Regional Director,[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1) and then Vice President, of the [Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America](/wiki/Science_Fiction_and_Fantasy_Writers_of_America "Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America").[[12]](#cite_note-beware-12) With Victoria Strauss, she founded Writer Beware, a "watchdog" group that is part of SFWA that warns aspiring writers about the dangers of scam agents, editors, and publishers.[[12]](#cite_note-beware-12) Writer Beware was founded in 1998, and has assisted law enforcement and civil authorities in tracking and shutting down writing scams.[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1)[[13]](#cite_note-SFWA_Obit-13)

On April 19, 2013, Crispin was named the 2013 Grandmaster by the International Association of Media Tie-In Writers (IAMTW).[[1]](#cite_note-WashPostObit-1)

Her final completed work being *Time Horse*.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Ridan Publishing Controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ann_C._Crispin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ridan Publishing Controversy")]

On October 5, 2012, Crispin posted on Facebook about her publishing company, Ridan Publishing, and its failure to communicate and pay Crispin her quarterly royalties. "Even though StarBridge came out on December 5, 2011, I have never received a royalty payment from Ridan," Ann said in her Facebook post. Ridan Publishing was owned by Robin Sullivan, wife of fantasy author Michael J. Sullivan, the author of the successful Riyria Revelations and several other series. This was a particularly important time to receive these funds, as Crispin was going through chemotherapy and needed funds to pay for the procedure.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ann_C._Crispin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

On September 3, 2013, StarTrek.com posted Crispin's farewell message after her prolonged illness [bladder cancer](/wiki/Bladder_cancer "Bladder cancer") deteriorated and became terminal. In the message Crispin said, "I want to thank you all for your good wishes and prayers. I fear my condition is deteriorating. I am doing the best I can to be positive but I probably don't have an awful lot of time left. I want you all to know that I am receiving excellent care and am surrounded by family and friends."[[15]](#cite_note-15)

She died only three days later, at 63.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ann_C._Crispin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Works")]

* **[The Han Solo Trilogy](/wiki/The_Han_Solo_Trilogy "The Han Solo Trilogy")**
  1. *[The Paradise Snare](/wiki/The_Paradise_Snare "The Paradise Snare")* (1997), [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-553-57415-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-553-57415-9 "Special:BookSources/0-553-57415-9")
  2. *[The Hutt Gambit](/wiki/The_Hutt_Gambit "The Hutt Gambit")* (1997), [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-553-57416-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-553-57416-7 "Special:BookSources/0-553-57416-7")
  3. *[Rebel Dawn](/wiki/Rebel_Dawn "Rebel Dawn")* (1997), [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-553-57417-5](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-553-57417-5 "Special:BookSources/0-553-57417-5")
  + Also available as *Star Wars: The Han Solo Omnibus* (2000)
* ***Star Wars* short stories**
  + "Play It Again, Figrin D'an" (in *[Tales from the Mos Eisley Cantina](/wiki/Tales_from_the_Mos_Eisley_Cantina "Tales from the Mos Eisley Cantina")*, 1995)
  + "Skin Deep" (in *[Tales from Jabba's Palace](/wiki/Tales_from_Jabba%27s_Palace "Tales from Jabba's Palace")*, 1996)
* **StarBridge novel series**
  1. *StarBridge* (1989)
  2. *Silent Dances* (1990) (with Kathleen O'Malley)
  3. *Shadow World* (1991) (with Jannean Elliott)
  4. *Serpent's Gift* (1992) (with Deborah A. Marshall)
  5. *Silent Songs* (1994) (with Kathleen O'Malley)
  6. *Ancestor's World* (1996) (with T. Jackson King)
  7. *Voices of Chaos* (1998) (with Ru Emerson)
* ***[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek "Star Trek")***
  + *[Yesterday's Son](/wiki/Yesterday%27s_Son "Yesterday's Son")* (1983)
  + *[Time for Yesterday](/wiki/Time_for_Yesterday "Time for Yesterday")* (1988)
  + *The Eyes of the Beholders* (1990)
  + *[Sarek](/wiki/Sarek_(novel) "Sarek (novel)")* (1994)
  + *Star Trek: Enter the Wolves* (2001) (with [Howard Weinstein](/wiki/Howard_Weinstein_(writer) "Howard Weinstein (writer)")) [WildStorm Comics](/wiki/WildStorm "WildStorm")
  + *Sand and Stars: Signature Edition* (2004)
* **Witch World**
  + *Gryphon's Eyrie* (1984) (with Andre Norton)
  + *Songsmith* (1992) (with Andre Norton)
* ***V***
  + *V* (1984)
  + *V: East Coast Crisis* (1984) (with Howard Weinstein)
  + *V: Death Tide* (1984) (with Deborah A. Marshall)
* **The Exiles of Boq’urain**
  + *Storms Of Destiny* (2005)
  + *Future projects: Exiles of Boq'urain Trilogy · Book 2 & 3*
* **Miscellaneous**
  + *Sylvester* (1985)
  + *Alien Resurrection* (1997) (with Kathleen O'Malley)
  + *[Pirates of the Caribbean: The Price of Freedom](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_The_Price_of_Freedom "Pirates of the Caribbean: The Price of Freedom")* (2011)
